steps.text: Perform a retrospective power analysis when using Scenario B to ensure that the survey has adequate statistical power to detect a survey unit that should not have passed.
Drop or re-cast language indicating that Scenario B is not as desirable as Scenario A.
Show through retrospective power analysis that the data strongly support the null hypothesis.

preconditions.text: Scenario B fails to reject the null hypothesis

constraints.text: Type II errors must be properly controlled for in Scenario B to confirm evidence in favor of the null hypothesis.

steps.text: State a description of the standard uncertainty for each input parameter of the final calculation.
Determine whether or not any of the parameters are correlated.
Calculate the combined standard uncertainty using the individual standard uncertainties and the estimated covariances.

preconditions.text: Individual standard measurement uncertainties for each parameter are introduced
Correlation of parameters has been established

steps.text: Provide detailed examples of how to calculate the CSU for different measurements/methods.
Provide a fully worked out example of determining an MDC in Chapter 6.

Emphasize that regardless of the difficulty of calculating or estimating uncertainty of a measurement, each measurement should have an associated uncertainty that is used in the final standard combined uncertainty.
Determine if the average concentration of radionuclide of concern over the entire area is below the Derived Concentration Guideline Levels (DCGLw) using statistical tests.
Compare individual concentration measurements to the DCGLEMC for compliance evaluation.
Investigate the area of relatively high concentrations.
Use the Unity Rule to ensure that the total dose or risk meets the release criteria.
Include a separate term in the calculation for each area of elevated activity.

preconditions.text: A measurement from the survey unit is equal to or greater than the DCGLEMC.
Elevated levels of residual radioactive material are found in an isolated area in addition to residual radioactive material distributed relatively uniformly across the survey unit.
There is more than one area of elevated activity.

steps.text: Determine the elevated area (primary area) that contributes the most to the total dose or risk.

List performance specifications for each instrument in Tables H.2-H.8 (Pages H-59 – H-72), including efficiency, energy range, energy linearity, resolution, signal to noise ratio, spectral deconvolution, dead time, specialized operator/radiochemistry training, transmission efficiency, isotopic fractionation effects, mass linearity, resolution, and sensitivity.

preconditions.text: To the extent practicable and appropriate for the purpose of implementing guidance

steps.text: Change an area's classification from Class 1 to Class 2.
Change an area's classification from Class 1 or Class 2 to Class 3.

preconditions.text: Ensure existing data (from the Historical Site Assessment [HSA], scoping surveys, or characterization surveys) provide a high degree of confidence that no individual measurement would exceed the derived concentration guideline level (DCGLW).

Ensure existing data (from the Historical Site Assessment [HSA], scoping surveys, or characterization surveys) provide a high degree of confidence that there is either no residual radioactive material or that any levels of residual radioactive material are a small fraction of the DCGLW.

constraints.text: Justifications for changing an area's classification may be appropriate based on the outcome of the Data Quality Objectives (DQO) process.

steps.text: Make Revision 2 available for public comment.
Incorporate suggested improvements as appropriate based on the public comment review.

Identify whether the inclusion and proposed implementation of scan-only surveys (Section 5.3.6.1 and Section 8.5) is appropriate, adequate and clear, especially the discussion on sampling for scan-only measurement method validation or verification.

preconditions.text: Input from the SAB should be sought for any significant changes, including those currently proposed for Revision 2.

constraints.text: Scientific concepts remaining materially unchanged from Revision 1 (e.g., the use of non-parametric statistics, Scenario A) do not require review at this time.
The MARSSIM document does not address volumetric or subsurface soils.

steps.text: Comment on the rule-of-thumb for determining when use of MARSSIM may not be appropriate for survey units containing discrete radioactive particles (Section 4.12.8 and Appendix O.5).
Comment on whether the description of updated measurement methods and instrumentation information (Chapter 6 and Appendix H) is useful, appropriate and clear.
Comment on whether the additional optional methodology for the use of Ranked Set Sampling (Appendix E) for hard-to-detect radionuclides is useful, appropriate and clear.

constraints.text: Survey designs must match the assumptions made during modeling, otherwise, the survey design does not meet the requirements of the action level.

The Ranked Set Sampling methodology requires a close, reasonable and provable correlation between an easy-to-measure attribute of the sample (e.g., soil sample size distribution) and the activity level of a hard-to-detect radionuclide.
